title: Classic ring doughnuts
subtitle: Yeasted doughnuts, fried and glazed
excerpt: Classic ring doughnuts are made from a yeasted, enriched dough, proved until puffy, then fried in hot oil and glazed with a thin sugar icing while still warm. The crumb is tender and slightly chewy. The glaze sets to a thin, crackly shell. The method takes about two and a half hours, most of which is proving time.
sourceNotes: Fried yeasted doughs appear across European and American baking traditions from at least the early nineteenth century. The ring doughnut shape, with its central hole, is generally attributed to American cooking from the mid-nineteenth century; one origin story places it in Rockport, Maine. Ring doughnuts are documented in American domestic cookery books from the 1870s onward and became commercially standardised in the early twentieth century. The basic enriched yeast dough formula is consistent across historical versions.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Doughnuts are best eaten within 4 hours of frying.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: deep-fried at 180 degrees C oil temperature; no oven required; fry for 60 to 90 seconds per side until deep gold

content.text: Oil temperature is the critical variable in doughnut frying. Too cool, below 170 degrees C, and the doughnuts absorb oil and become greasy. Too hot, above 195 degrees C, and the outside colours before the centre is cooked through. The target is 180 degrees C: the doughnut should take 60 to 90 seconds per side to turn a deep amber, which gives enough time for the interior to cook fully. Use a thermometer; relying on visual cues alone for oil temperature is unreliable.

In a large bowl or stand mixer, combine 400 g plain flour, 7 g yeast, 50 g caster sugar, and half a teaspoon of salt. Add 180 ml warm milk, 2 beaten eggs, and 50 g softened butter. Mix and knead with the dough hook for 8 minutes, or by hand for 12 minutes, until smooth and elastic. Cover and leave to bulk ferment at room temperature for 1 hour until doubled. Knock back, then roll out on a lightly floured surface to 1.5 cm thick. Cut with a large round cutter (8 cm) and a small one (2.5 cm) for the holes. Place on floured baking paper and prove for 40 to 45 minutes until visibly puffed.

Heat the oil in a deep, heavy pan to 180 degrees C; use a thermometer. Fry the doughnuts in batches of 2 to 3, sliding them into the oil carefully using a fish slice. Fry for 60 to 90 seconds on each side until deep amber. Remove with a slotted spoon and drain briefly on kitchen paper. Make the glaze by whisking 200 g icing sugar with 3 tablespoons of boiling water and half a teaspoon of vanilla until smooth. Dip the warm doughnuts in the glaze and place on a rack to set, about 10 minutes.

items.solution: The oil was below 180 degrees C. When the temperature drops between batches, wait for it to recover before adding the next. Greasy doughnuts cannot be salvaged; temperature control prevents them.

items.problem: Doughnuts are dark outside but raw inside
items.solution: The oil was too hot. Reduce to 175 to 180 degrees C and fry more slowly; 60 to 90 seconds per side is the minimum to cook the interior of a 1.5 cm thick doughnut.
